preFix.title: Profiteroles
preFix.subtitle: Choux pastry puffs filled with cream and drenched in chocolate
preFix.excerpt: Profiteroles are choux pastry puffs filled with whipped cream and drenched in a warm chocolate sauce. The pastry is made on the hob, piped into small rounds, and baked at a high temperature so the steam inside causes them to puff dramatically. They collapse only slightly on cooling. Fill just before serving.
preFix.sourceNotes: Choux pastry is documented in French professional patisserie from at least the sixteenth century. The profiterole, a small choux ball filled with cream or ice cream and served with chocolate sauce, appears in French domestic and restaurant cooking from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) lists several choux preparations using this basic formula. The method of cooking the paste on the hob before adding eggs is the defining technique of choux pastry and distinguishes it from all other pastry types.

content.text: Choux pastry is made in two stages on the hob. First, butter and water are brought to a boil, then flour is added all at once and beaten over the heat until the paste pulls away from the pan as a clean ball. It then has to cool for a few minutes before the eggs go in: adding eggs to a hot paste means they will partially cook. The eggs are added one at a time, beating fully between each, until the paste is smooth and glossy and falls from a spoon in a slow, heavy ribbon.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. In a medium saucepan, bring 150 ml water, 60 g diced butter, and a quarter teaspoon of salt to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat and tip in 100 g sifted plain flour all at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the paste comes together and pulls cleanly from the sides of the pan. Return to a low heat and beat for 1 minute more to dry the paste slightly. Transfer to a large bowl and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs gradually, a little at a time, until the paste is smooth, glossy, and falls from the spoon in a slow, heavy ribbon. You may not need all the egg.

Spoon the paste into a piping bag fitted with a 1 cm plain nozzle. Pipe rounds about 3 cm across onto the trays, spacing them 4 cm apart. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until deep gold all over, dry to the touch, and hollow-sounding when tapped on the base. Do not open the oven door in the first 20 minutes. Split each puff with a small sharp knife and leave on a rack until completely cool before filling.

Bring 150 ml double cream to just below a simmer. Pour over 150 g broken dark chocolate and leave for 1 minute, then stir until smooth. Add 25 g butter and stir until glossy. Fill each puff with a teaspoon of whipped cream. Pile onto a serving plate and pour the warm chocolate sauce over just before serving.

items.problem: The puffs collapsed when removed from the oven
items.solution: They were underbaked. The puffs must be fully dry and hollow-sounding before they come out. If they collapse, return to the oven for a further 5 minutes with the door slightly ajar to allow steam to escape.

items.problem: The paste would not come together and remained wet
items.solution: Too much egg was added. Add the beaten egg gradually and stop as soon as the paste reaches a slow, dropping consistency. Different-sized eggs mean the recipe may not need all 3.
